scenarios.subject: Your Package Could Not Be Delivered — Action Required
scenarios.body:
Dear Customer,

We attempted to deliver your package today but were unable to complete delivery.

Package ID: FX-293847162
Status: Held at facility

To reschedule, please pay the outstanding customs fee of $3.50:

Pay Now → http://fedex-tracking.net/pay

Your package will be returned to sender if not claimed within 48 hours.

FedEx Support
scenarios.explanation: This is phishing. FedEx's real domain is fedex.com, not fedex-tracking.net. FedEx does not charge customers customs fees via a payment link in an email. The small $3.50 fee is designed to seem harmless so you enter your card details without thinking.
redFlags: Small fee designed to seem harmless
Real FedEx never requests payment via email links

scenarios.subject: Security alert: New sign-in on Windows
scenarios.body:
Your Google Account

A new sign-in to your Google Account was just detected.

When: June 14, 2026, 2:14 PM GMT
Device: Windows PC
Location: Chicago, Illinois

If this was you, no action is needed.

If you don't recognize this, review your account activity at myaccount.google.com/security.

The Google Accounts Team
scenarios.explanation: This is a legitimate Google security alert. The sender is @accounts.google.com, which is a real Google domain. The email contains no suspicious links — it directs you to myaccount.google.com. There is no urgency, no threats, and no request to click an unknown link.

scenarios.subject: Congratulations! You're our 1 millionth customer — Claim your free MacBook
scenarios.body:
CONGRATULATIONS!

You were just selected as our 1,000,000th customer of the month and have been chosen to receive a brand new MacBook Pro M4 worth $2,499.

To claim your free MacBook, confirm your shipping address and pay only the $9.95 shipping fee:

Claim MacBook → http://amazon-shipping-rewards.com/claim

This offer expires in 24 hours.

Amazon Rewards Team
scenarios.explanation: This is a classic prize scam. Amazon's real domain is amazon.com, not amazon-shipping-rewards.com. Amazon does not run customer milestone giveaways like this. Any 'free' prize that requires you to pay shipping or a small fee is a scam — once you enter your card details, attackers steal them.
redFlags: Unsolicited 'prize' you never entered to receive
Small fee required to claim 'free' item
Real companies don't ask for shipping fees on giveaways

scenarios.subject: Alex shared a folder with you
scenarios.body:
Hi,

Alex Johnson shared a folder with you on Dropbox.

Project Files — Summer 2026

Open Folder → dropbox.com/sh/abc123xyz

Dropbox
scenarios.explanation: This looks like a legitimate Dropbox sharing notification. The sender is @dropbox.com (the real domain) and the link goes to dropbox.com. Dropbox regularly sends notifications when someone shares files. Always hover over links to confirm they go to the real domain before clicking.

scenarios.subject: Action Required: Update Your Direct Deposit by Friday
scenarios.body:
Dear Employee,

Our payroll system is being upgraded. All employees must update their direct deposit information before Friday to avoid a delay in payment.

Please complete the update form here:
https://your-company-payroll.com/update-banking

You will need your bank account number and routing number.

HR Department
scenarios.explanation: This is phishing. Your real HR team would never ask you to update banking information through an external link to a generic domain like 'your-company-payroll.com'. This attack redirects your paycheck to the attacker's account. Always verify payroll changes by contacting HR directly through your company's internal systems.
redFlags: Requests bank account and routing numbers via a link
Payroll changes should go through official internal systems only

scenarios.subject: Order Confirmation: Apple iPad Pro $1,299.00
scenarios.body:
Thank you for your order!

Order #114-9482711-3829
Apple iPad Pro 12.9" — $1,299.00
Shipping to: 482 Oak Lane

If you did NOT place this order, cancel immediately:

CANCEL ORDER → http://amaz0n-orders.com/cancel

Order ships in 2 hours.

Amazon
scenarios.explanation: This is phishing. Look carefully — the domain is amaz0n-orders.com with a zero instead of an 'o'. The scam uses a fake expensive order to panic you into clicking the 'cancel' link, which leads to a fake login page. Real Amazon orders can always be reviewed at amazon.com/orders.
redFlags: Large fake order to cause panic
Real Amazon never threatens shipping deadlines this way

scenarios.subject: Invitation: Team Standup @ Wed Jun 17, 2026 10am - 10:30am
scenarios.body:
You have been invited to the following event:

Team Standup
Wednesday, June 17, 2026 · 10:00am – 10:30am EST

Organizer: priya.shah@company.com
Location: Google Meet (link in event)

Yes / Maybe / No — respond in Google Calendar.

Google Calendar
scenarios.explanation: This is a legitimate Google Calendar invitation. The sender is @google.com, the format matches real Google Calendar invites, and the organizer is your coworker's actual address. No suspicious links and no requests for credentials.

scenarios.subject: Netflix: We couldn't process your payment
scenarios.body:
Hi,

We were unable to validate your billing information for your Netflix subscription.

Your account will be paused unless we receive updated payment details:

Update Payment → http://netflix-account-services.com/update

If you do not update within 48 hours, your subscription will be canceled.

Netflix Member Services
scenarios.explanation: This is phishing. Netflix's real domain is netflix.com, not netflix-account-services.com. Real Netflix billing issues are also handled by signing into netflix.com directly, never through an external link in an email.
redFlags: Uses fear of losing service to rush you

scenarios.subject: Your Apple ID has been locked for security reasons
scenarios.body:
Dear Customer,

Your Apple ID has been locked because we detected unusual activity.

For your security, please confirm your identity within 24 hours:

Unlock Apple ID → http://apple-id-support.com/unlock

If you do not respond, your Apple ID will be permanently disabled and all iCloud data will be deleted.

Apple Support
scenarios.explanation: This is phishing. Apple's real domain is apple.com, not apple-id-support.com. Apple does not threaten to delete your iCloud data over a locked account. Real Apple ID recovery happens at appleid.apple.com or in the Settings app on your device.

scenarios.subject: [org/repo] @priya-s requested your review on PR #482
scenarios.body:
Hi,

@priya-s requested your review on Pull Request #482:

'Refactor payment retry logic'

View Pull Request: https://github.com/org/repo/pull/482

You can also reply to this email directly to comment on the PR.

— GitHub
scenarios.explanation: This is a legitimate GitHub notification. The sender is @github.com (the real domain), the link goes to github.com, and the format matches GitHub's standard review-request email. GitHub regularly sends notifications like this.

scenarios.subject: Invoice_June_2026.pdf has been shared with you
scenarios.body:
A document has been shared with you.

Document: Invoice_June_2026.pdf
From: accounting@yourcompany.com

View Document → http://secure-document-share.com/view

Password: 2026Invoice (case sensitive)

This document is encrypted for your security.

Secure Document Share
scenarios.explanation: This is phishing. The 'encrypted document with password' pattern is a common phishing technique — the password makes it seem secure but actually tricks you into entering credentials on a fake login page. Real document sharing happens through services your company has approved, not random domains.
redFlags: Password in the same email (defeats the security claim)
Generic invoice topic to entice business users

scenarios.subject: You have a new voicemail (00:42)
scenarios.body:
You have received a new voicemail.

From: +1 (415) 555-0182
Duration: 00:42
Received: June 14, 2026 at 9:14 AM

Listen → http://voip-message-service.net/play

VoIP Message Service
scenarios.explanation: This is phishing. Unsolicited 'voicemail' emails from unknown VoIP services are a common phishing lure — the link leads to a malware download or fake login page. If you use a real voicemail-to-email service, you'd already recognize the sender.

scenarios.subject: Your Discover Weekly is ready 🎵
scenarios.body:
Hi,

Your new Discover Weekly playlist is here, with 30 songs picked just for you.

Listen now in the Spotify app.

Not in the mood? You can change your weekly email preferences in Account → Notifications.

— Spotify
scenarios.explanation: This is a legitimate Spotify notification. The sender is @spotify.com (the real domain), the message contains no clickable suspicious links, and it directs you to use the Spotify app you already have installed.

scenarios.subject: Marcus Chen wants to add you on LinkedIn
scenarios.body:
Hi,

Marcus Chen, Senior Recruiter at Meta, wants to connect with you on LinkedIn.

Accept Invitation → http://linkedln.com/accept/8472

You have 7 days to respond before this invitation expires.

LinkedIn
scenarios.explanation: Look closely — the domain is linkedln.com, not linkedin.com. The 'i' has been replaced with an 'l'. Real LinkedIn invitations are visible in the app or at linkedin.com, and they don't 'expire' in a way that requires email-link action.
redFlags: Big-name company in invitation as bait
Real LinkedIn invitations are managed in the app

scenarios.subject: Norton: Your subscription will auto-renew tomorrow for $399.99
scenarios.body:
Dear Customer,

Your Norton 360 subscription will automatically renew tomorrow for $399.99.

If you did not authorize this renewal, call our billing department immediately:

+1 (888) 555-0114

Or cancel online: http://nortonantivirus-billing.com/cancel

Norton Billing
scenarios.explanation: This is a classic 'fake invoice' scam. Norton's real domain is norton.com. The inflated price ($399.99 — much more than real Norton) is meant to alarm you into calling. The phone number connects you to scammers who use remote-access tools to steal data or pressure 'refunds'.
redFlags: Pressure to call a phone number

scenarios.subject: Your Amazon.com order of 'AirPods Pro' has shipped
scenarios.body:
Hello,

Your Amazon.com order has shipped.

Apple AirPods Pro (2nd generation) — $249.00

Arriving: Tuesday, June 16

Track at amazon.com/orders

Amazon.com
scenarios.explanation: This is a legitimate Amazon shipping confirmation. The sender is @amazon.com (the real domain), the item matches a recent order, and it directs you to amazon.com/orders without any urgent action or external link.

scenarios.subject: Please DocuSign: 2026_Q2_Contract.pdf
scenarios.body:
You have a document to review and sign.

From: legal@partner-firm.com
Document: 2026_Q2_Contract.pdf

Review Document → http://docusign-esign.net/review

This document expires in 7 days.

DocuSign
scenarios.explanation: This is phishing. DocuSign's real domain is docusign.net or docusign.com — note the legitimate one would be docusign.net itself, not docusign-esign.net. Attackers commonly impersonate DocuSign because business users routinely sign documents this way. Always verify the sender domain carefully.
redFlags: Real DocuSign emails come from docusign.net

scenarios.subject: You have received a 2-page fax
scenarios.body:
A new fax has been received in your inbox.

From: +1 (212) 555-0119
Pages: 2
Received: June 14, 2026 at 11:42 AM

View Fax → http://efax-online-service.com/view

eFax Online Service
scenarios.explanation: This is phishing. Unsolicited 'fax received' emails from unfamiliar services are a phishing lure — the link leads to malware or a credential-stealing page. If you use a real fax service, you'd recognize its actual domain.

scenarios.subject: @jordan-w sent you a message in #design-reviews
scenarios.body:
@jordan-w:

'Hey, can you take a look at the new home screen mocks before the meeting?'

Reply in Slack → slack.com

You are receiving this because you are not currently active in Slack.

Slack
scenarios.explanation: This is a legitimate Slack notification. The sender is @slack.com (the real domain), the format matches Slack's mention notifications, and the link goes to slack.com. Slack sends emails like this when you're not active and someone mentions you.

scenarios.subject: DHL: We attempted to deliver your parcel today
scenarios.body:
Dear Customer,

We attempted to deliver your parcel today (Tracking #DHL-7281-9382) but no one was present.

A redelivery fee of $2.99 is required:

Pay & Reschedule → http://dhl-express-delivery.com/redeliver

Unclaimed parcels are returned after 5 days.

DHL Customer Service
scenarios.explanation: This is phishing. DHL's real domain is dhl.com, not dhl-express-delivery.com. DHL does not charge customers small 'redelivery fees' through email payment links — they reattempt delivery automatically or leave a card. The tiny fee is designed to bypass your suspicion so you enter card details.

scenarios.subject: Final notice: Your $1,247 refund expires soon
scenarios.body:
Dear Former Customer,

Our records show you are owed a refund of $1,247.86 from your old internet service account.

To claim this refund, please confirm your identity and bank account details:

Claim Refund → http://telecom-services-billing.com/refund

A small administrative fee of $35 is required to process the wire transfer.

Unclaimed refunds are returned to the state treasury after 14 days.

Telecom Refund Department
scenarios.explanation: This is a refund scam. Legitimate companies never ask you to pay a fee to receive a refund — refunds are returned through the original payment method. The vague 'old internet account' lacks specifics on purpose so it could apply to anyone. The goal is to steal both your bank details and the $35 fee.
redFlags: Pay fee to receive refund (always a scam)
Asks for bank account details via external link

scenarios.subject: Welcome to The Verge — confirm your subscription
scenarios.body:
Hi,

Thanks for subscribing to The Verge daily newsletter!

Click below to confirm your email and start receiving your daily digest:

Confirm Subscription → theverge.com/confirm/8472921

If you didn't subscribe, you can ignore this email.

— The Verge
scenarios.explanation: This is a legitimate newsletter subscription confirmation. The sender is @theverge.com (the real domain), the link goes to theverge.com, and the language is normal and non-urgent. Newsletters routinely send confirmation emails like this to prevent spam signups.

scenarios.subject: Your mailbox is 98% full — Messages will bounce
scenarios.body:
Dear User,

Your Office 365 mailbox is 98% full. Incoming messages will start bouncing in 24 hours.

Upgrade storage now (free):

Upgrade Storage → http://office365-mailservices.com/upgrade

You will be asked to verify your Microsoft 365 credentials.

Office 365 Administrator
scenarios.explanation: This is phishing. Microsoft's real domains are microsoft.com or office.com — not office365-mailservices.com. Real mailbox-full warnings come through Outlook itself, not external links. The 'upgrade' page is a fake login designed to steal Microsoft 365 credentials.
redFlags: Real Microsoft warnings appear inside Outlook

scenarios.subject: iCloud Storage Full — Action Required
scenarios.body:
Hi,

Your iCloud storage is full. To continue receiving photos and backups, please add a payment method or your data may be lost.

Update Payment → http://icloud-support-center.com/billing

Apple Support
scenarios.explanation: This is phishing. Apple's real domain is apple.com or icloud.com, not icloud-support-center.com. Real iCloud storage warnings appear on your Apple devices in Settings, not as emails with external payment links. The 'data loss' threat is fearmongering.
redFlags: Real iCloud warnings appear in device Settings

scenarios.subject: Your Google verification code is 482917
scenarios.body:
Google

482917 is your Google verification code.

Don't share this code with anyone. Google will never ask for it.

If you didn't request this code, someone may be trying to access your account. Visit myaccount.google.com to secure it.

— Google
scenarios.explanation: This is a legitimate Google 2-factor verification code. The sender is @accounts.google.com (a real Google domain), the message is short and factual, contains no clickable suspicious links, and explicitly warns you not to share the code. Real codes look like this.
